steps.title: Identify the slope of line l using perpendicularity
steps.content:
When two lines are perpendicular, their slopes multiply to $-1$. Since line $k$ has slope $m$, the slope of line $l$ is:
$$m_{\text{line } l} \cdot m = -1$$$$m_{\text{line } l} = -\frac{1}{m}$$

steps.title: Use point-slope form to write the equation of line l
Substitute $x = 3$ to find the $y$-coordinate
steps.content:
Plug $x = 3$ into the equation of line $l$:
$$y - 9 = -\frac{1}{m}(3 - 2)$$$$y - 9 = -\frac{1}{m}$$$$y = 9 - \frac{1}{m}$$

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — '\((3, 9 - \frac{1}{m})\)': Moving one unit right from \((2,9)\) along a line of slope \(-\frac{1}{m}\) gives \(y = 9 - \frac{1}{m}\). ✓
  • Choice B — '\((3, 9 + \frac{1}{m})\)': This would require slope \(+\frac{1}{m}\), but the perpendicular slope is \(-\frac{1}{m}\) (negative). ✗
  • Choice C — '\((3, 9 - m)\)': Slope \(m > 1\) so \(-m\) and \(-\frac{1}{m}\) are different; \(-m\) is the slope of a line parallel to \(k\) reflected, not perpendicular. ✗
  • Choice D — '\((3, 9 + m)\)': Same error as C with an extra sign flip — not the perpendicular slope. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
